data.title: How Likely Is It That The Bottom Has Not Yet Been Reached?
data.description:
The tariff pause seems to have restored a glimmer of confidence to the markets; at least we are no longer experiencing double-digit declines in 48 hours. However, the underlying problem remains and Trump does not seem willing to change his modus operandi. I believe that the purpose of the tariffs is simply to negotiate more favorable trade agreements, but his statements may not be conducive to achieving this goal:

I'm telling you, these countries are calling us up, kissing my ass. They are. They are dying to make a deal.

I'm not sure this is the best way to negotiate, but who am I to judge. The market also still has many doubts and volatility is very high, so everything can change very quickly. No one knows if the bottom has already been reached, but we can look to the past for some insight into the future. Every crisis has different triggers, but there are similarities that can help us.

Death Cross and Yield Curve

In recent days, there has been much talk about the S&P 500 experiencing a death cross after several years. For those who don't know, a death cross occurs when the 50-day moving average breaks below the 200-day moving average.

Typically, this signal is interpreted as a potential continuation of the downward trend. But is it really that reliable? Perhaps not, and I'll explain why.

To begin with, I'm not a big fan of technical analysis, so I wouldn't base an investment thesis solely on looking at a chart. However, there are some signals that are actually more useful than others, but I don't think the death cross is one of the significant ones. There are two main reasons for this: it's not statistically significant and it's often lagging.

Throughout history, there have been many more death crosses than it might seem when considering only the last few years. In 50 years, the S&P 500 has recorded 24 death crosses, and obviously not all of them predicted a recession. In particular, only in 46% of cases did the index fall further after the death cross signal. If anything, given that the chances of the index rising after the death cross are higher than those of a further decline, this indicator is more bullish than bearish. In 100 years of data, in the 20 days following the death cross, the S&P 500 fell 52% of the time: almost the same predictive power as a coin toss.

The death cross is a lagging signal, so it does not tell you if the bottom has been reached, only that the trend has reversed based on the data obtained up to that point. The point is that we do not want to know what has happened, but what will happen. If you wait for the golden cross (the opposite of the death cross) before investing, you risk leaving a large portion of your gains on the table. Similarly, if you start selling everything when there is a death cross, you risk selling right near the bottom.

I don't understand why people give so much importance to the death cross; it's probably because of its dramatic name. When something is called a “death cross,” you expect the worst, but this is almost never the case. Only on three occasions has the death cross lived up to its name: 1981, 2000, and 2007. In those cases, the S&P 500 lost 21%, 45%, and 55% respectively after the signal.

If you think about it, it is no coincidence that the death cross proved reliable in 2000 and 2007, as both were serious crises that lasted 1-2 years. When there is a flash crash like in 2020, being a lagging indicator is not very helpful, because by the time it occurs, the bottom has already been reached. From this point of view, there may be similarities with what we are experiencing today: a rapid crisis that could be resolved quickly if tariffs were no longer the topic of the day.

Overall, I would not consider the death cross as a signal that tells us whether the bottom has been reached. A different case is the spread between 10-year and 2-year government bonds.

Many believe that the sign of a recession is when the yield on 2-year government bonds exceeds that on 10-year bonds, resulting in a downward sloping yield curve. In reality, this is only the first trigger, but the recession occurs when the yield curve re-inverts and the 10-year rate exceeds the 2-year rate again. So technically there was no false signal, simply the first inversion lasted much longer than expected due to out-of-control inflation. Now the re-inversion process is underway, we have well exceeded the 0 threshold, and from here on the pitfalls begin. It typically takes a few months/quarters for a recession to occur after the re-inversion, and we are exactly at that point. While two months ago I could not have imagined what the trigger for the recession would be, today I have some ideas. But I want to make one thing clear.

It is not certain that we will have a recession in the coming months/quarters; no indicator can show this with certainty. However, the yield curve has proven to be reliable since the 1990s, and frankly, I cannot tell you why today should be any different. In the event of a recession, unemployment will rise, household consumption will fall, and a lower bottom than that reached in early April will be inevitable.

The resilience of American households

After causing panic with my yield curve speech, I now want to show you something positive. There are never only negative or positive signs, and it is right to look at the other side of the coin.

First of all, it is important to understand that recession is an inevitable phase of the economic cycle and cannot be avoided, so the sooner you accept this, the better. Trying to predict it and shorting the market will not make you rich in most cases; the opposite is more likely. Don't watch “The Big Short” too often.

That said, recession is not always as catastrophic as it was during the housing bubble.

On average, the S&P 500 lost 32% peak to trough during a recession. If we consider the median, and therefore exclude the most extreme values, it fell by 27% peak to trough. So, if we were facing a “normal” recession, we are already halfway there in terms of the decline. Even if the market were to fall by 15% in the coming months, I don't think it would be the end of the world.

Logically, we cannot know in advance if there will be a recession and how deep it will be, but I don't think it's appropriate to use a rare case like 2008-2009 (-57%) as a benchmark. That said, even if we were facing a new 2008, it doesn't mean we would suffer for years and years.

That recession was the longest since World War II and lasted 18 months. By averaging down during the recession, you could have reached the break-even point even sooner. Clearly, those who invested everything at the all-time high took years and years to recover their losses, but this is a basic mistake that should be avoided. If I am interested in a company or an index, I never invest everything at once, but divide the investment into 3-4 tranches, so I have the liquidity to average down.

Even in the worst-case scenario, after a year and a half of suffering, your portfolio will probably be back on track. If you have bet on companies with a terrible business model, then it's a different story, but that's up to you.

So the recession should be seen for what it is: an inevitable period of economic difficulty that will probably be resolved after a year or so. Either way, compared to 2008, American households seem to be in better shape financially, and that's important.

Household financial obligations have a lower weight on disposable personal income than in 2008, and credit card defaults are at a level that is not cause for concern. These figures have worsened in recent quarters, but this is nowhere near the levels seen in past recessions. If American households prove resilient, then consumption will not fall significantly in the event of a recession, and consequently the magnitude of the recession should not be much greater than the median.

The indicator that has never been wrong, so far

No one has a crystal ball, but there are indicators that are more reliable than others. A few weeks ago, I presented one that is little known but has so far proved infallible: the ratio between the yield on 3M T-Bills and the spread between high-yield bonds and the respective risk-free rate.

This indicator exploits bond market sentiment, as it spots the onset of a recession in advance. When the spread widens, it means that financial operators are demanding a higher premium for the risk they are taking, which is a typical behavior of those who do not trust the near future. At the same time, when the yield on 3M T-Bills falls, it means that the market is pricing in more rate cuts by the Fed, probably to cope with a more difficult economic environment.

When this ratio breaks below the 100-week moving average, it signals a recession is on the way.

This is the chart from a few weeks ago, and as you can see, there was a small downward break. However, in my article discussing this, I was cautious, as it could have been a false positive. For the signal to be valid, there must be a strong downward break.

Here's what happened after two weeks: I would say that today the break is evident. This time I also marked green lines that correspond to previous market bottoms, from which we can draw the following conclusion.

If the indicator proves reliable again, the bottom is still quite far away. Every time it has been reached, the black line was almost at the bottom (~0.10%), and today we are not even halfway there: there could still be room for a further drop.

Conclusion

Interest rates are fueling chaos, the yield curve and bond market spreads are signaling a recession. However, US households are not as heavily indebted as they were in 2008, which leads me to believe that they can maintain a certain degree of resilience even in the event of a recession. I would rule out a catastrophic scenario.

If I had to answer the question in the title, I would say that there is a higher probability that the bottom has not yet been reached. It has never been so difficult to make predictions because all it takes is one post to cause the S&P 500 to crash or rebound. The crucial issue is tariffs; without them, we probably would not have had this decline. The proof of this statement lies in the fact that the market recorded one of the best days in its history when Trump announced a pause.

That said, tariffs are here to stay, and I believe they will continue to be a widely used tool for Trump in negotiations. As long as this is the case, it is unlikely that the stock market will regain investor confidence. In particular, China is not willing to back down and has several levers at its disposal to win the challenge against the US; I discussed this in a previous article. The parties need to move closer together, but they are still quite far apart.

The ball is in China's court. China needs to make a deal with us; we don't have to make a deal with them. There's no difference between China and any other country except they are much larger, and China wants what we have... the American consumer. They need our money.

White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt

But if this is the tone, how can an agreement be reached?

data.title: Novo Nordisk: A Price Too Low To Ignore
data.description:
The healthcare sector might have been among the least affected by the market rout that has been 2025 so far, but the Danish weight loss treatments' giant, Novo Nordisk (NVO) hasn't been that lucky. The stock is down by over 32% year-to-date [YTD], compared with the sector's largely flat performance, as indicated by the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLV) (see chart below).

This is entirely in contrast with my upgrade of the stock to Buy last December, in line with the company's robust fundamentals and its attractive market multiples. Here, I look at what has gone wrong with Novo Nordisk in the recent months and whether there's still a Buy case for it.

Drug trial results disappoint investors

A key reason for NVO's drop is the disappointing trial results for its obesity and diabetes treatment CagriSema. The first big price fall came after the company released its phase 3 trial results in late December last year. NVO plunged by 18% on the day.

This was puzzling on the face of it since the trial met its primary endpoint. Also, a notable 22.7% weight loss was observed for patients after 68 weeks of using it, compared with 16.1% for semaglutide, which is marketed as the company's biggest brands, Ozempic and Wegovy.

But there was a good reason for the price development. The company management had expected weight loss to be at 25%, and even the solid actual number fell short of the claims. How confident the management was in achieving the target was evident during its earnings call following the company's third quarter (Q3 2024) results. At the time, BNP Paribas Exane analyst, Richard Parkes, asked the management how they reconcile the results of the earlier clinical trials for the treatment (see slide below for details on the results).

To this, the management said the following:

So first of all, it's important to call out we have no new data. And therefore, our confidence remains to be the same. The way that we think about this is that we have three sets of data to look at from a modeling perspective. One is Phases 1 and 2 for [indiscernible] mono therapy, then Phase 1, 2 for obesity and CagriSema and thirdly, the Phase 2 mile for CagriSema in Type 2 diabetes. We can then apply our models, I would say, on this based on our extensive knowledge and experience within the obesity space, and we arrive then at the 25% weight loss."

Essentially, the stock tanked less due to the actual results and more because they numbers didn't turn out to be as positive as the company had hoped. That this followed disappointing results from the Monlunabant trials earlier last year likely only compounded investor diffidence. Then last month, the results of yet another phase three trial for CagriSema also fell short of expectation. The stock tanked again, albeit by a smaller 9.4%.

The tariff challenge

While trial outcomes are the big reason for the stock's decline, the spectre of tariffs has played a role too. Besides impacting stock markets as such, there is a specific uncertainty relating to its home country Denmark. In one word, Greenland. At present Greenland is Denmark's autonomous territory, but the present US government wants to acquire it or face potentially punishing tariffs. This is already creating a backlash, with Danish consumers boycotting US made products.

It remains to be seen how this story evolves, but until then, some uncertainty remains along with the risk of disruptions for Novo Nordisk as tariffs will increase prices. The company's key manufacturing facility is still in Denmark, though it does produce in the US too and with its acquisition of contract manufacturer Catalent, it can further expand its production in the country.

Financials and market multiples

In the meantime, though, the company remains in a good financial shape. As the table below shows, it saw 20%+ growth in both sales and earnings in 2024. Novo Nordisk does expect a bit of pullback in growth figures in 2025, as the outlook table below shows, but the prospects for the year still look rather good on their own.

In fact, the outlook lends itself positively to the net earnings projections too. To estimate Novo Nordisk's, two assumptions are made. First, that the company's ratio of net income to operating income in 2025 remains the same as in 2024 at 78.7% and second, the operating profit will grow at the midpoint of the guidance range. This results in a net profit of USD 18.6 billion in the year, a 23% increase from 2024.

With this net profit projection, NVO's forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is now at just 15.3x. The fact that the forward P/E is more than half its level the last I checked underlines just how much the stock has fallen. Moreover, even NVO's five-year average at 31.4x is way higher than the present levels. Basically the numbers indicate that the stock can more than double this year.

What next?

Will NVO actually double this year is the real question, though. So far, it appears unlikely. Not with the stock markets looking the way they have through much of the year. Even though the healthcare sector has been less impacted, Novo Nordisk is facing its own challenges.

Repeated disappointments with regards to new weight loss drug trials last year increase the pressure on the company to produce more blockbuster treatments like Ozempic and Wegovy. For now though, it does have some time on its side, with these treatments' patents expiring only in 2033 and 2032 respectively.

But even Novo Nordisk were to find replacement treatments soon enough, the tariff challenge is hard to look past right now. This is specifically so since the current US administration doesn't view Denmark, where the company manufactures, positively due to its stance on Greenland. Just uncertainty on this front can be bad for the stock, and in the event of a negative outcome between the two countries, earnings will impacted negatively as the company is left with no choice but either increase its prices or take a hit on margins.

For now though, the extent of drop in NVO's price is just too much to ignore. If all turns out well for the company once the dust settles, the stock can start skyrocketing. This isn't the time to put a significant chunk of the portfolio into the stock going by the balance of risk and potential reward, but having a position in it can still be a positive over time. I'm retaining a Buy rating on NVO.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Viant Technology: Riding The AI And CTV Wave, But Not Ready For Prime Time
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Viant Technology (NASDAQ:DSP) provides programmatic advertising through its cloud-based demand-side platform ('DSP'). Through its platform, advertisers get to optimise ad campaigns across multiple platforms from one unified interface, improving campaign efficiency and decision-making.

While such advertising platforms are not uncommon, the company differentiates itself from closed platforms like Google and Meta with real-time auctions for ad placements across multiple channels like websites, apps, and Connected TV ('CTV'). The process is transparent and empowers advertisers to make a call on whether their ad spending is aligned with their selling objectives.

Financially, the company just turned profitable, but its historical trend of profitability has been inconsistent. Despite its inconsistency in bottom-line performance, the company maintains a strong balance sheet characterised by low debt and high cash reserves. Viant also benefited from an additional stream of non-operating interest income due to a large part of its cash reserves stashed away in low-risk, short-term investments.

One of Viant's strongest growth areas in FY2024 is attributed to advertisers' reported surge in preference for CTV advertising. The company intends to ride on such a trend and improve its offerings through its privacy-friendly household ID technology and leveraging AI through the launch of 'ViantAI'.

Viant's use of AI extends across all channels, not just CTV. If fully materialised, the entire process of programmatic advertising is expected to be "fully autonomous", enhancing productivity to a whole new level.

Despite Viant's promising growth plans in CTV and the use of AI, the broader digital advertising industry is still led by the Big Tech, which is more resourced to outpace smaller players like Viant in the scale of AI-powered advertising solutions.

The stock is only slightly undervalued. Investors should hold and wait for a larger margin of safety amid the uncertainty in market leadership against its much larger competitors.

Company Overview

Viant Technology is a digital advertising technology company that provides a cloud-based demand-side platform, allowing advertisers to self-serve in the planning and execution of their advertising campaigns. Data-driven insights from these campaigns running across multiple channels can also be accessed within a single unified platform.

With the launch of "ViantAI" in 2024, the company enhanced its DSP offering with AI.

The company differentiates itself from major "walled garden" advertising platforms like Meta, Google, and Amazon by providing transparent ad buying through real-time auctions across a diverse range of ad spaces that include websites, apps, and Connected TV platforms. In contrast, a "walled garden" model operates like "black boxes", where advertisers have limited visibility on the decisions made on how or where their ads are displayed.

Financials (Income Statement)

Its revenue has been increasing consistently as inferred from its latest annual report, but it only turned profitable recently:

Historically, despite the recent profitability, the company's profitability trend since 2018 has not been consistent:

Financials (Balance Sheet)

Its working capital might have dipped a little, but it's still very favourable, consisting mostly of liquid cash reserves:

The company accumulates very little debt and operates with a net cash position. Its debt is only a fraction of its annual EBITDA, meaning the company can pay off these debts within one year if it chooses to.

Its massive cash reserves are stashed away in short-term investments that allow the company to benefit from an additional stream of non-operating interest income:

Financials (Cash Flow)

The company's free cash flow has been historically consistent, except for a temporary dip in 2022.

Referring to the gradient of the linear trendline, the company increases its free cash flow by about $5.85MM per year. Relative to the latest FCF of $49.4MM, if the current trend continues, Viant can be expected to grow by about 12% per year. We will use this growth rate in the valuation of the company.

Valuation

The following values, taken from Seeking Alpha and other sources, will be used in the intrinsic value ('IV') calculation using the discounted free cash flow model:

  • Starting FCF of $49.3MM as referenced from the latest Dec 2024 period.
  • The company is expected to accumulate 65M shares outstanding at the end of this year, which we will assume remains materially constant thereafter.

  • WACC as referenced from GuruFocus:

  • Five-year growth: As noted earlier, Viant's historical growth in FCF is consistent at roughly 12% per year. We will assume the company can maintain such growth in the next 5 years.

With these assumptions, Viant appears slightly undervalued.

Programmatic Advertising

EMARKETER provides a comprehensive walkthrough on programmatic advertising, which is the core offering of Viant:

In essence, here are the main steps:

  1. The advertiser builds the campaign on the DSPs. They choose who they intend to reach, how much they are willing to pay for this reach, and how their Ads will look.
  2. Through supply-side platforms ('SSPs'), DSPs connect to potential publishers through websites, apps, and streaming services.
  3. Whenever users visit one of these publishing platforms, like websites or apps, a real-time auction instantly occurs behind the scenes. As multiple DSPs bid, the highest bidder wins, and their ad is shown.
  4. Over time, performance metrics like views, clicks, and conversions are collected to gain insights on which campaigns work best. With this feedback, the campaign's strategy can be refined over time.

AI-Powered Automation (ViantAI)

Viant's release of ViantAI massively enhanced programmatic advertising by using AI to automate the process through 4 different "phases":

An overwhelming majority of bidding is done through "AI bidding" as opposed to a traditional rules-based approach, delivering the most cost-effective bid results in real-time.

Advertisers leverage "AI Planning" to build campaigns in seconds using natural language prompts. Not only is this fast, but the level of complexity and optimisation delivered is enterprise-level, which is normally not achievable through human efforts:

Traditionally, advertisers had to go through static, manual campaign performance reports that were generated periodically, which could be both time-consuming and mentally straining. Planned for launch in Q2 2025, "AI Measurement & Analysis" offers on-demand insights that deliver campaign performance insights instantly.

Perhaps the most ambitious goal of Variant's overall leverage of AI is in "AI-decisioning", which is planned for launch in H2 2025. If the goal is fully materialised, the entire process of programmatic advertising (as described in the previous section) will be "fully autonomous" – from planning and bidding to measurement and optimisation:

Connect TV ("CTV")

CTVs are essentially the devices or environments that offer streaming services from content providers like Netflix and Disney+. Compared to normal TV where contents are scheduled and broadcast, CTV's on-demand feature enables tracking of viewing habits and preferences, allowing for more precise advertising.

During the latest earnings call, the management reported a major shift in advertisers' preference for CTV advertising on Variant's platform:

This is one of the largest growth areas that the company intends to grow further together with other technologies like "Household ID".

Household IDs

Cookies track personal browsing preferences on the internet. While this is effective for advertisers to launch targeted campaigns, consumers pushed back on such intrusive data collection practices.

Due to privacy issues that arise from cookies' access to usage preferences at the personal level, the company uses household IDs that map usage preferences at the household level, which may consist of multiple persons and, hence, are more aggregated and do not infringe on privacy.

Viant's household ID technology, which advertisers reportedly rely on for their advertising campaigns, is also proprietary, meaning satisfied users are likely to stay with Viant. The technology greatly enhanced the digital ad space that is available for purchase from advertisers, compared to prevailing alternative identifiers.

Alternative identifiers include third-party cookies, which are reportedly deprecated in usage.

This depreciation is a structural change due to regulatory pressures resulting in platform restrictions:

Risks

I like Viant's forward-looking growth plans, leveraging the rise of CTV and AI to improve its offering in programmatic advertising. However, the broader digital advertising industry is still led by the big tech companies, and it appears unlikely to abate in the near future:

Viant might have offered a more transparent alternative to Big Tech's "wall-garden" approach, but at the end of the day, in my view, campaign results matter more. If the closed advertising platform offered by the big techs can help advertisers close more sales, they are more likely to invest more in it.

The leverage of AI by Viant is a promising strategy, but not exclusive to Viant. Big Techs are also leveraging AI, and in my opinion, they have more resources to scale and eventually outpace other smaller competitors.

Conclusion

Viant, with its impressive growth in the CTV space and the launch of ViantAI, demonstrated innovation in its growth outlook. However, the digital advertising landscape is competitive and currently led by the Big Tech companies, meaning innovation alone may not be enough to rise above the competition.

In my view, its ability to translate these innovations into a material competitive advantage is questionable.

It is only slightly overvalued, meaning it does not present a short-term, deep-value play opportunity.

In the long run, long-term investors should hold and wait for a larger margin of safety while observing whether Viant presents clearer signs of sustained profitability and competitive advantage.

data.title: Alcoa's Q1 2025 Review: Tariff Hurts, But Game Isn't Over
data.description:
My Thesis Update

My previous article on Alcoa Corporation (NYSE:AA) hasn't aged well, as after an initial short-lived rally in late 2024, the stock rolled back and is currently trading at a ~75% drawdown (relative to all-time high).

Having a solid vertically integrated operational structure, which spans from bauxite mining and alumina refining to primary aluminum smelting, Alcoa is a real stalwart in the global aluminum industry, but it's clearly losing big from the tariff war and economic uncertainty. But the Q1 numbers that the firm released yesterday don't show the negatives just yet. While I think Alcoa management's cautious note regarding the ~ $100 million in net annual negative impact on the business (assuming current LME and premium levels) is a major drag for the next few quarters, the rise in costs will likely be transferred to US buyers. That's why I'd be looking for doubling down in case AA keeps dropping from here, hence my "Buy" rating reiteration.

Alcoa's Q1 2025 Results Quick Review

While AA's top line dipped 3% QoQ to ~$3.4 billion because of lower alumina shipments and pricing, the firm's net income more than doubled to ~$548 million, or $2.07 per share - this is why adjusted net income came in even stronger at $568 million, or $2.15 per share, meaningfully beating the Street's consensus estimates for the quarter, according to Seeking Alpha:

Thanks to "higher realized aluminum prices and lower intersegment profit eliminations", based on the CFO's commentary, the company's adjusted EBITDA also saw a healthy sequential increase of $178 million to $855 million, which is a solid recovery on a QoQ basis, in my opinion.

Alcoa partially refinanced its old debt with a successful execution of a $1 billion debt offering in Australia, thereby extending maturities and lowering the after-tax interest expense, and easing the pressure on future earnings. The current capital allocation framework is targeting an adjusted net debt range of $1-1.5 billion to achieve investment-grade metrics, so good shareholders' returns will likely follow only progress towards this goal (right now, the analysts forecast less than 2% in dividend yield for the next year).

Despite a typical for AA working capital build up during 1H, the Q1 cash flow from operations was positive $75 million, supporting the liquidity on the balance sheet (the cash item went up by 5.6% YoY).

The Tariffs Hurt

As I said in the beginning, the tariffs are going to hurt Alcoa's business in the next few quarters - the first signs were already seen in some places of the just-released report. For example, the "Other costs" category had ~$20 million related to the U.S. Section 232 tariffs impacting Canadian aluminum starting March 12th:

Included in other costs are approximately $20 million for U.S. Section 232 tariffs of 25% on aluminum imports from Canada, which became effective on March 12.

Source: From Alcoa's earnings call

In mid-March, when those tariffs came into effect, the exemption previously granted to Canadian aluminum imports was removed, so since ~70% of Alcoa's Canadian aluminum production is destined for U.S. customers, all this volume is now subject to a 25% tariff, potentailly translating into an annual gross cost of $400-425 million for the firm. For an entity that makes $10-12 billion in annual sales, it might not look like much (just ~4%), but given the operating leverage and potential headwind to margins, the bottom line is going to take a hit at a higher rate.

Based on updated guidance, Alcoa estimates that for Q2 2025 (the first full quarter under the new tariff regime), it's going to see an unfavorable QoQ impact of ~$90 million, specifically from the above Section 232 tariffs on Canadian imports. The total unfavorable impact guided for the Aluminum segment in Q2 is $105 million, which also "includes $15 million in restart costs for the San Ciprian smelter". The subsequent reciprocal tariffs involving Canada, Mexico, and China will pose another cost (smaller, yet notable for the resulting EPS).

Yet the Midwest premium (the U.S. regional price), which should have risen to ~$0.40-$0.45/lb (~$880-$990/ton) to fully reflect the 25% tariff and incentivize shipments from other regions (based on analysts expectations that the management cited on the call), the actual premium only reached ~$0.39/lb on average for Q1. But I should note here that the Q2 premium will likely be much higher than that, given the real-time developments on the market:

The most likely step that the US government will take, in my view, is a renewed Canadian exemption, because Alcoa and other aluminium giants won't restart US production en masse any time soon - such decisions come at a high cost and uncertanity, and tariffs - even at 25% - can't make it happen, in my understanding.

So the hit that Alcoa has taken yesterday after the Q1 release is an interesting high-risk, high-reward opportunity.

Alcoa Stock's Valuation Update

On a log scale, to smooth out the massive spike in mid-2024, Alcoa's EV/EBITDA ratio today is trading at its lowest level in the past 5-10 years, according to YCharts:

This isn't a justification to initiate a position as is, but it clearly shows the extent of pessimism in the market today.

Considering a 20% reduction in today's consensus estimate for AA's EPS for FY2025, we'll get an implied price-to-earnings of just ~6.92x on a forward basis. This is still 2-3 times below the medians currently seen for the Materials sector.

In my October 2024 article, I wrote that Alcoa was trading at a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 9.4x at the time, which was approximately the median of its sector. Now, given the stock price slump and even after assigning a huge discount to next year's EPS figure, we find AA trading at a valuation that is one-third lower.

Risks To My "Buy" Rating

Beyond just tariffs, the aluminum price retreated from its Q4 2024 highs during Q1 2025, and it keeps going lower because of increased liquidity from Chinese refinery ramp-ups (plus normalized production elsewhere):

The market may see even lower pricing as the surplus is rising, according to Goldman Sachs, reciprocal and expected sector-specific tariffs will likely result in a 15% increase in the US effective tariff rate and a lower China's GDP, hence giving the aluminum sector a lot of additional supply after the refinery ramp-ups.

We now forecast a global aluminium market surplus of 580kt in 2025 (vs. 76kt deficit prior). This is the result of a downwards revision to our global total aluminium demand growth forecast to 1.1/2.3% y/y in 2025/2026 (vs. 2.6/2.4% y/y prior) as we expect a hit from weaker global GDP growth.

Source: Goldman Sachs [proprietary source], April 14

If so, the pricing will likely hit Alcoa's margins, and its EV/EBITDA ratio will first be lower, and then shoot up like in mid-2024 (as the denominator of that proportion will be cut). Keep that risk in mind while doing your own valuation analysis of Alcoa.

Your Takeaway

I know risks are surrounding Alcoa from multiple sides: tariffs, aluminum surplus, loss-making projects, depressed sentiment, etc. But I believe it's a high-risk, high-reward opportunity for the brave at today's stock prices. If Trump wants to rebuild domestic production of any sort, then that effort will need a lot of aluminium. The current tariffs don't make construction accessible, so the policies will likely be revised (perhaps in a way favorable to Alcoa). If so, the current undervaluation of the AA stock is what's giving it a lot of upside, in my view. In the longer term, as Alcoa progresses with deleveraging and the next phases for exiting projects, the stock should reverse, in my view.

Personally, I'm not loading up just yet, waiting for a price action confirmation. But I leave my "Buy" unchanged for now.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: CLOs: Staying Nimble Amid Tariff Driven Volatility
data.description:
CLOs posted positive Q1 total returns as spreads widened and market volatility grew. Amid trade tensions and signs of economic slowdown, we prefer tranches higher in the capital stack.

In the first quarter, the VanEck CLO ETF (CLOI) outperformed its benchmark, the J.P. Morgan CLO Index, by 11bps (1.18% vs 1.07%), and the VanEck AA-BB CLO ETF (CLOB) outperformed its benchmark, the J.P. Morgan CLOIE Balanced Mezzanine Index by 10bps (1.12% vs 1.02%).

CLO spreads widened through the quarter, and “Liberation Day” has added significant uncertainty to the outlook. Given signs of economic weakening in the US and the prospects of a global trade war, we prefer tranche purchases higher in the capital stack, with selective purchases of shorter spread-duration assets for lower-rated credits. However, wider spreads may begin to offer opportunities for long-term investors, and we maintain the ability to shift further into lower-rated tranches given our overall portfolio positioning higher up the capital stack. A robust bottom-up approach to security selection that looks beyond a tranche credit rating remains key, given the significant tail risks to the fundamental backdrop and increased dispersion in performance among vintages.

Market Update

In March, CLOs generated marginally positive total returns as the impact of carry outweighed negative price returns. This month marked the 24th consecutive month of positive total returns. However, it was the lowest total return since March 2023 as BBB to B-rated tranches generated negative total returns. All tranches posted positive returns for the quarter. Investor sentiment has grown more cautious amid a backdrop of an escalating trade war with the potential risks of an economic slowdown and stubbornly sticky inflation. During the month, all eyes were on April 2, when the Trump administration was expected to unveil the next steps of their tariff regime. Ultimately, the administration announced “reciprocal tariffs” on more than 180 countries and territories to varying degrees, including a 20% tariff on the EU and 34% on China. The narrative coming out of the Federal Reserve following its March meeting was mixed, as it downgraded economic growth to 1.7% from 2.1% and increased core inflation expectations to 2.8% from 2.5%. Despite that, the Fed signaled a somewhat dovish tone, as the dot plot still indicated two rate cuts this year while it is reducing tapering its balance sheet. However, the dot plot showed that four members now see no change in rates, up from just one member in January. The 5-year Treasury rate traded 7 bps lower, and the 10-year Treasury rate was unchanged during the month.

CLOs outperformed bank loans and high yield corporates but underperformed investment grade corporates and the agg as longer duration exposure contributed positively.

Asset Class Q1 2025 Return (%) Yield to Worst (%) Spreads (BPS)
CLOs 1.07 5.73 177
CLOs IG 1.09 5.44 148
CLOs Mezz 1.02 7.48 347
AAA 1.08 5.17 123
AA 1.06 5.69 174
A 1.16 5.92 192
BBB 1.10 6.98 296
BB 0.75 11.35 725
U.S. Agg 2.79 4.64 41
Investment Grade Corporates 2.36 5.16 97
High Yield Bonds 0.94 7.73 355
Leveraged Loans 0.48 8.30 399
Source: JP Morgan and ICE Data Indices as of 3/31/2025. CLOs represented by J.P. Morgan Collateralized Loan Obligation Index, CLOs IG represented by J.P. Morgan Collateralized Loan Obligation IG Index, CLOs Mezz represented by J.P. Morgan Collateralized Loan Obligation Balanced Mezzanine Index, AAA Rated CLOs represented by J.P. Morgan CLO AAA Index, AA Rated CLOs represented by J.P. Morgan CLO AA Index, A Rated CLOs represented by J.P. Morgan CLO A Index, BBB Rated CLOs represented by J.P. Morgan CLO BBB Index, BB Rated CLOs represented by J.P. Morgan CLO BB Index, US Agg is represented by the ICE BofA US Broad Market, Investment Grade Corporates represented by ICE BofA US Corporate Index, High Yield Bonds represented by ICE BofA US High Yield Index and Leveraged Loans represented by JP Morgan Leveraged Loan Index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

CLO new issue supply decreased month-over-month with $16.8bn pricing, compared to $18.4bn in February. Refinancing and reset activity slowed but continued at a rapid pace in March with $35.4bn pricing, after $37.7bn in February. First quarter total issuance of $150.2bn is the fastest issuance to start a year on record and the second-largest quarterly issuance in CLO 2.0 history behind 4Q 2024.

In the secondary market, TRACE supply increased month-over-month, with $22.7bn of volume in March compared to $13.6bn in February. Investment grade volumes increased to $18.5bn from $10.1bn and below investment grade volumes increased to $4.2bn from $3.5bn. Meanwhile, total BWIC volume increased to $7.7bn from $5.1bn in February and was the highest since March 2020.

In March, gross institutional loan issuance was $35.7bn, following $44.2bn in February. The primary market continued to cool in March amid rising volatility and softening investor sentiment. Opportunistic transactions declined as the number of loans trading at or above par, typical candidates for repricings and refinancings, fell further. One bright spot was LBO and M&A transactions, which totaled $20 billion for the month and contributed to a more balanced technical environment. Retail loan funds saw net outflows of $6.2bn. The average bid of the Morningstar LSTA Leveraged Loan Index decreased 84bp to end the month at 96.31, the largest monthly decline since September 2022. The percentage of loans pricing at par or above decreased to 10.3% from 35.8%; the percentage of loans pricing between 95 and par increased to 73.9% from 51.5%; the percentage of loans pricing below 90 increased to 9.0% from 7.1% and loans pricing below 80 increased to 3.2% from 3.1%.

The trailing twelve-month default rate within the Morningstar US Leveraged Loan Index increased 1bp month-over-month to 0.82%. As measured by JP Morgan, the default rate including distressed exchanges decreased 7bp, but remains elevated at 3.86%. Activity has been elevated as borrowers with unsustainable capital structures endeavored to manage their liabilities and avoid the bankruptcy process through liability management exercises, keeping the “official” default rate lower than otherwise. We anticipate the default rate to remain below historical averages in the near term for the leveraged loan market as a result. Nonetheless, our expectations are that defaults, including distressed exchanges, will remain above the long-term historical average of ~3%, with the path for the default rate uncertain given rapidly changing trade policy and US CLO spreads widened across the capital stack.

Portfolio Strategy

Despite rate cuts in 2024, the borrowing rate for leveraged loan companies remains high following rate increases from central banks in 2022 and 2023. While the Fed initiated a series of rate cuts starting in September 2024, inflation remains above the Fed’s target and the labor market remains robust, although signs of slowing growth are starting to creep up. The Fed has paused further action as they await more information on the path of inflation and employment, which may result from the implementation of tariffs and more robust immigration policies. Market expectations with respect to Fed cuts moved slightly higher in March, with the market now expecting three to four rate cuts in 2025. However, the Fed appears to be in a very tenuous position, having to balance both sides of their dual mandate of maximum employment and stable prices during a period where stagflation is looking ever more likely. Cuts will ultimately provide relief for more stressed borrowers, but the path and timing of more cuts are highly uncertain.

The rally in CLO prices we’ve seen since the lows in 4Q 2022 has cracked, with prices declining across the capital stack in March and throughout the beginning of April. The average price across all tranches is now below par for the first time since January 2024. We have also begun selling out of positions higher in the capital stack to raise cash as wider spreads lower in the capital stack are starting to look more attractive for longer-term investors. If spreads were to widen further, we maintain the ability to shift further into lower rated tranches, given our overall portfolio positioning higher up the capital stack.

As markets have sold off in recent weeks, buying in the secondary market has become much more attractive. That said, buying in the primary market also remains attractive, even when taking spread duration into account. CLO equity arbitrage in 2024 was supported by continued liability spread tightening. While recent volatility has driven CLO debt spreads wider, average loan prices have declined materially from the highs in late January, making CLO creation more attractive. Despite that, high levels of amortization and call volumes resulted in marginally negative net AAA supply for 2024 and flat net supply through 1Q 2025.

Average Annual Total Returns* (%)

As of March 31, 2025
1 MO 3 MO YTD 1 YR 3 YR 5 YR 10 YR LIFE06/21/22
CLOI (NAV) 0.08 1.18 1.18 6.98 - - - 7.92
CLOI (Share Price) -0.12 1.04 1.04 6.72 - - - 7.86
J.P. Morgan Collateralized Loan Obligation Index 0.05 1.07 1.07 7.00 - - - 8.14

* Returns less than one year are not annualized.

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Performance may be lower or higher than performance data quoted. Please call 800.826.2333 or visit ETF & Mutual Fund Manager | VanEck for performance current to the most recent month ended.

CLOI’s gross expense ratio is 0.40% and the total expense ratio is 0.40%. Van Eck Associates Corporation (the “Adviser”) will pay all expenses of the Fund, except for the fee payment under the investment management agreement, acquired fund fees and expenses, interest expense, offering costs, trading expenses, taxes and extraordinary expenses. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Adviser has agreed to pay the offering costs until at least May 1, 2025. “Other Expenses” have been restated to reflect current fees.

Average Annual Total Returns* (%)

As of March 31, 2025
1 MO 3 MO YTD 1 YR 3 YR 5 YR 10 YR LIFE09/24/24
CLOB (NAV) -0.23 1.12 1.12 - - - - 4.04
CLOB (Share Price) -0.28 1.08 1.08 - - - - 4.01
J.P. Morgan CLOIE Balanced Mezzanine Index -0.36 1.02 1.02 - - - - 3.90

* Returns less than one year are not annualized.

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Performance may be lower or higher than performance data quoted. Please call 800.826.2333 or visit ETF & Mutual Fund Manager | VanEck for performance current to the most recent month ended.

CLOB’s gross expense ratio is 0.45% and the total expense ratio is 0.45%. Van Eck Associates Corporation (the “Adviser”) will pay all expenses of the Fund, except for the fee payment under the investment management agreement, acquired fund fees and expenses, interest expense, offering costs, trading expenses, taxes and extraordinary expenses. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Adviser has agreed to pay the offering costs until at least May 1, 2026. “Other Expenses” have been restated to reflect current fees.

Outlook

Clearly the ground has shifted coming out of “Liberation Day,” with the implications and the ultimate impact to the economy still uncertain. Many questions remain related to the impacts on employment and sentiment; if and when the Fed will react and how large of an impact any actions to support the economy will have; and, ultimately, how other countries around the world will react and/or retaliate. Looking ahead, we see continued geopolitical uncertainty and market volatility as tariff negotiations continue over the next few weeks and months.

Through just the first nine days of April, we have seen the Trump administration announce “reciprocal tariffs” on more than 180 countries and territories to varying degrees, including a 20% tariff on the EU and 34% on China, China retaliating with 84% tariffs of its own, followed by the administration “immediately” raising U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports to 145% while simultaneously pausing increased tariff rates above 10% for other countries for 90 days. While the path of trade policy is highly uncertain, what is highly likely is that even a minimum increase of 10% in tariffs will increase inflation and lead to a significant economic slowdown, or more likely, a recession in the United States and the rest of the world. Unemployment will also increase – the only question is when and to what level. In addition, given the increased prospects of a stagflationary environment, we do not expect the Fed to come to the rescue. At present, our view is that given expectations of sticky or higher inflation, the Fed will most likely only start to lower rates when unemployment increases above 5%.

In a world where interest rates will be higher for longer and credit spreads wider, floating rate assets continue to be attractive. We are looking to rotate down from AAA/AA tranches to A/BBB and opportunistically adding some BB-rated tranches where allowed. Historically, BBB-, BB- and B-rated CLOs outperform in the years following significant fixed income underperformance, and we expect them to be among the top performing fixed income asset classes.

That said, we see spreads and yields attractive under most market scenarios over the next twelve months. Notwithstanding the shorter-term tailwinds, we believe wider spreads are beginning to offer attractive entry points for longer term investors. However, a robust bottom-up approach to security selection remains key given the significant tail risks to the fundamental backdrop and a market bifurcated between vintages and, relatedly, between deals in and out of their reinvestment periods. Given the dispersion seen in the loan market, certain CLO portfolios holding weaker credits may eventually experience impairments to the lowest-rated debt tranches. As a result, vintage, portfolio, and manager selection remain key.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: El Al Israel Airlines: Speculative Buy Opportunity With Risk Of Competition
data.description:
El Al Israel Airlines Ltd. (OTC:ELALF) has surged 45.5% since my last report for investors in January. The stock has not yet reached my price target of $4.32, but I do believe it is time for an update on the stock as dynamics that supported significantly higher stock prices are changing. It should be noted that the OTC listing has gained 45% since my last coverage bringing the stock price to $3.71, but the listing of El Al at the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange “only” gained 20%. This is driven by lower volumes on the OTC ticker which may result in price making that at times is not reflective of the price changes for the underlying instrument.

In this report, I will be discussing the most recent results, update on the risk and opportunities and update my price target for El Al stock.

El Al Shows Huge Earnings Growth

Revenues increased strongly in 2024 to $3.4 billion. This increase was driven by an 11.8% increase in capacity and higher unit revenues as well as an increase in cargo activities. Total operating expenses grew by 18.9%, which was higher than the capacity expansion. System unit costs excluding fuel increased by 9.6%. The operating profit increased by 189.1% to $772.7 million with net earnings growing from $116.7 million to $544.9 million.

The Risks For El Al Airlines

The results of El Al Airlines have without doubt been impressive and the stock price performance year-to-date also shows as much. If we look at the main carriers in the US, we see that those have all declined. That is driven by the trade turmoil.

El Al’s stock performance is diverging from that. One simple reason would be that it is not a US carrier and therefore it is less affected by the trade turmoil. The other reason is simply because it had a unique positioning in connecting Israel to the world since the war in Gaza started. When the war started, airlines pulled out of Israel leaving El Al to service demand. El Al had a tailwind from that including increased demand for flying reservists into Israel as well as cargo.

That may very well become the risk for El Al going forward. The war is not over nor do we see strong signs that tensions in the region will ease in the near-term. However, airlines are restarting flights to Israel. United (UAL) restarted flights in March and Delta restarted on the first of April. American Airlines has not yet announced a resumption of service, but a flurry of European operators have. So, the marketplace is expected to look significantly different in 2025 and that may not be in the benefit of El Al.

El Al Stock Price Target Revised Downward

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For El Al, I am basing my price target on the stock price at the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange converted to USD. That price target is $3.83 which implies 29% upside. However, against the OTC listing the upside is merely 3.2%. So, the OTC listing offers a less compelling investment at this point, but the underlying instrument remains attractive.

While I do see downward risk to earnings in the years ahead as the marketplace normalizes for El Al leading to lower unit revenues, the company seems to be much better capitalized to service its debt. I do note that the company needs to show strong cost control as the company is unlikely to offset cost growth by strong unit revenue growth in the new set up were international carriers will service part of the air travel demand.

My stock price target has decreased from $4.32 to $3.83 to align with earnings pressure exerted by other airlines re-entering the market.

Conclusion: El Al Israel Airlines Stock Has Upside But Risk Is Increasing

I do believe that El Al Airlines stock continues to have upside, but with international airlines restarting services to Israel there will be pressure on unit revenues and earnings providing a significant downside risk to the estimates. So, the buy rating is speculative and I do have to point out that upside has been lowered for 2025 and shifted to the right thereafter.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MSCI: A Top Pick Amid Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Within the financial sector, the stock of MSCI (NYSE:MSCI) has been one of the outperformers amid the market turmoil. Their asset-light business, high client switching costs, and no direct impact from tariffs are preferred attributes for investors during this market environment.

MSCI's core business is licensing market indices to asset managers. For the most part, they charge a variable fee to the funds licensing their indices based on their AUM. At the same time, they offer market data solutions to financial institutions. Half of their client base are asset managers, but they also serve banks, hedge funds, and consultants.

Since the market peak of February 19, the S&P 500 has dropped approximately -14.25%, followed by the financial sector -10.83%, and MSCI, which has outperformed, dropping -8.13%. Although MSCI is not immune to a GDP slowdown, the degree to which it is affected is likely much lower than, for example, a bank, which might incur excess provisions for loan losses. At the same time, MSCI has more than 7,100 institutional clients across more than 100 countries, and their global indices are popular among foreign asset managers.

MSCI Ability To Generate Free Cash Flow

MSCI is an asset-light business that does not require much investment in maintenance capex and has a high net income margin, with the most recent one of 38.83%. With a strong revenue rise over the years, the firm has consolidated a CAGR of 13%, which has also translated to the growth in free cash flow with a CAGR of 16%. As a consequence, the company is able to deliver these returns in a mix of dividends and share repurchases. Over the past six years, the share count has been reduced by -12.2%, and the quarterly dividend has been increased by 210%.

MSCI: Shares Remain Undervalued

In my previous analysis of MSCI, I concluded that its shares were undervalued. Although the shares of MSCI haven't dropped considerably compared to the rest of the market, the undervaluation still holds and has slightly expanded.Currently, MSCI trades at 30.9x price-to-free cash flow, which is a -27% difference from the five-year historical average of 42.2x.

At the same time, based on a discounted cash flow model, the shares have an intrinsic value of $638, which implies an upside potential of 14.6% based on Tuesday's stock closing price. The model considers the revenue estimates for the next three years and thereafter assumes a 7.5% growth in the remaining years, holding the ratio averages steady. It also supposes a 5% perpetual growth rate of the free cash flow and a discount rate of 8.5% based on CAPM.

Risks From AUMs Dropping and Potential ESG Shift in Europe

With global asset levels dropping considerably, MSCI will probably experience an effect on its top line as asset managers would be required to pay less fees for licensing MSCI's indices. In 2024, asset-based fees were $657.501 million and grew 17.9% from the prior year due to higher AUMs. At the same time, financial institutions might be incentivized to reduce expenses, and one way to do this is by reducing the budget for subscriptions. Last year, MSCI achieved $2.114 billion in recurring subscriptions, which grew 13% YoY. Nonetheless, subscription cancellations saw a rise of $131.510 million during the year and were up 41.1% YoY.

For an asset manager, subscriptions are substantially easier to discontinue than index licensing, which might remain till the termination of a fund due to the complexity of changing a benchmark. Nonetheless, it is possible that seeking capital might become more complex in this environment, and funds to be launched by asset managers might be delayed or canceled.

Another risk is their Climate & ESG segment, which constituted 11.4% of the operating revenues in 2024. For this, MSCI has developed a large range of tools for asset managers to obtain ESG data and link it to their portfolios. Nonetheless, the future of ESG remains uncertain, with strong outflows in the United States.

The largest demand for ESG funds comes from Europe, which constitutes approximately 56% of the equity AUMs. Still, with the war in Ukraine and the lack of support from the Trump administration for European defense, Europeans have started to rethink their asset allocations and become more flexible. Germany, for example, has approved a spending package in military defense and infrastructure, exceeding 1% of their GDP. Weapons, for example, are negatively screened from most ESG funds, but asset managers, such as UBS, Allianz, and Danske Bank, have started to adjust their policies.

Takeaway

My previous ratings of MSCI have all been a strong buy. This is a business that is powerful in the Porter's Five Forces framework and has an enviable operating leverage with an operating margin of 53.72% that has considerably expanded through the years, when it was at 37.57% in 2015. Their ability to generate and grow free cash flow has been substantial through the years, and demonstrates the quality of the business. In addition, they have a shareholder-friendly management that has spent $3.764 billion in share repurchases over the past five years, to the point that the equity of the business became negative.

Although the drawdown isn't as appealing compared to the rest of the market, the valuation is now cheaper and continues to be undervalued. At the same time, if market drops continue, it is likely that MSCI won't be as affected as they don't have a direct impact from tariffs, and most of their revenue is recurrent. For the reasons mentioned above, I will continue to rate the shares of MSCI as a "strong buy."

data.title: Morgan Stanley Direct Lending: 10% Yield And 114% Dividend Coverage Make It A Buy
data.description:
High yield is sometimes associated with higher risk. While that may be true for some standard C Corporations, it’s not necessarily the case across all asset sectors. In particular, BDCs are by design structured to distribute 90% of their taxable income to shareholders such that the corporate entity doesn’t need to pay income taxes.

While individual shareholders need to pay taxes on their dividends received, that can be deferred if a BDC is held in a tax advantaged retirement account like an IRA or Roth IRA. Moreover, high income can be a great buffer against market volatility.

Such I find the case to be with Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL), which I last covered in January, highlighting its well-constructed and conservatively managed portfolio, which helps in these volatile times. Despite the stock price having declined in price, MSDL’s total return of -6% (including dividends) since my last piece is nearly 50% better than the 11.2% decline in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe. At the current price of $19.33, MSDL well in the bottom half of its 52-week range and carries a dividend yield well above 10%.

In this article, I revisit MSDL including its business fundamentals and valuation and discuss what makes it a great ‘Buy’ on the dip for value and income, so let’s dive in!

Why MSDL?

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending is a BDC that’s externally managed by Morgan Stanley (MS). This affiliation enables MSDL to leverage the investment bank’s extensive sponsor relationships and deal origination capabilities.

MSDL adopts a conservative lending approach toward U.S. middle market companies. This is reflected by 97% of its portfolio being in the form of first lien senior secured debt with a weighted average loan-to-value ratio of 40%. This helps to ensure a significant amount of equity to buffer against potential losses. Plus, MSDL’s investments have private equity sponsors or comparable market participants, which helps to ensure an additional layer of due diligence (since PE firms have more to lose) and management guidance for portfolio companies.

Moreover, MS has good alignment of interest with MSDL due to its 11% ownership stake. It also charges a below industry standard base management fee of 1.0%, comparing favorably to the 1.5% fee of industry bellwether, Ares Capital (ARCC). MSDL’s portfolio is also well diversified with 95% non-cyclical investments.

The average position size makes up just 50 basis points of the portfolio total with no borrower comprising over 2%. As shown below, the top sectors represent a blend of growth and defensive including software, insurance, commercial services & supplies, and IT services, which make up nearly half the portfolio.

It’s no secret that a decline in interest rates has impacted MSDL’s portfolio income, due to 99.6% of MSDL’s credit portfolio being tied to floating rate loans. This is reflected by NII per share declining by $0.10 on a YoY basis to $0.57 in Q4 2024. This is driven by the weighted average yield on cost declining by 160 bps from 12% at the end of 2023 to 10.4% at the end of last year. Importantly for income investors, MSDL’s NII continues to cover the regular $0.50 dividend rate with a 114% coverage ratio.

It’s worth noting that the silver lining to lower rates is that they have attracted more borrowers coming out of the woodwork. While the federal base interest rate declined by 100 basis points last year, MSDL’s portfolio grew by a robust 19% YoY to $3.8 billion fair value. This was driven by $574 million worth of net funded investment activity last year. As shown below, over 99% of new fundings over the past 5 quarters were in first-lien secured debt, bringing the total percentage of portfolio first lien from 94% to 97%.

At the same time, MSDL maintains solid portfolio credit quality with 98.3% of investments having an internal credit rating of 1 or 2 (on a scale from 1 to 4, with 1 being the highest quality). Since mid-2024, MSDL’s percentage of investments with a 3 or 4 rating declined by 20 basis points to 1.7%.

Plus, investments on non-accrual remain low at just 0.2% of portfolio cost. Measuring non-accrual as a percentage of portfolio cost is generally better than portfolio fair value, since the former is a better measure of long-term investment performance (i.e. results are not diluted by recent portfolio growth). Moreover, NAV per share remained steady, declining by just $0.02 sequentially to $20.81. As shown below, MSDL’s NAV/share is up significantly since its inception in 2023.

Risks to MSDL include the potential for lower interest rates should the economy hit a snag due to pressures from tariffs. Economic pressures could also lower borrower demand. In addition, MSDL’s PIK income has grown from $2 million in 2023 to $3 million last year, as shown below. PIK income refers to payment-in-kind, in which interest payments are not made by the borrower in cash but rather rolled into the balance of the loan. As one can imagine, PIK income is a riskier type of loan and I would monitor to see if PIK income rises relative to the overall portfolio in the coming quarters.

MSDL maintains a sound balance sheet with Baa3/BBB- credit ratings from Moody’s and Fitch. This is supported by a debt to equity ratio of 1.08x, which is higher by 9 basis points sequentially but far lower than the 2.0x statutory limit for BDCs.

Lastly, I continue to find MSDL to be appealing at the current price of $19.33 with a price-to-NAV of 0.93x, equating to a 7% discount from NAV. The discount seems to be too high given the conservatively managed portfolio comprised of first-lien loans, strong balance sheet and steady portfolio performance with a low non-accrual rate. Plus, with a dividend that has a 114% coverage ratio based on NII, MSDL offers a mix of value and high income.

As shown below, MSDL is the cheapest among other large BDC peers Ares Capital, Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), which carry P/NAV ratios in the 1.05 to 1.19x range.

Investor Takeaway

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending stands out as a compelling high-yield investment, especially in today’s volatile market. Despite a recent dip in share price, MSDL’s total return has outperformed the broader market. This is thanks to its high yield that’s backed by a conservatively managed, first-lien-focused portfolio and strong underwriting discipline.

Backed by Morgan Stanley’s deep sponsor network and a well-covered 10.3% dividend yield, MSDL offers both high income and buffers against portfolio losses. At its 7% discount to NAV and with minimal credit losses, MSDL presents an attractive opportunity for value and income investors at the current price.

data.title: Reel Relief: Escaping Economic Angst At The Movies
data.description:
With all that’s happening with tariff uncertainty, let’s lighten the mood a bit. How does a long holiday weekend with an afternoon trip to the movies sound? The Wall Street Horizon team is totally in. Springtime is here, the sun will rise again, and Hollywood is cranking out some gems. Join us.

Improving Numbers

According to Box Office Mojo by IMDbPro, “A Minecraft Movie” has revived 2025’s motion picture storyline. Through April 13, the Warner Bros. (WBD) film grossed over $280 million. It has led ticket sales for 10 days running through last Sunday, helping to bring this year’s US box office revenue to $1.85 billion, about on par with the same period in 2024. Through March, 2025 had lagged year-ago sales by more than 11%, per Box Office Mojo.1

Strong Sales Ahead? Or Will the Industry Lay an Egg?

All eyes are now on Easter weekend, which includes Good Friday - a bank holiday and an opportunity for families to get out to the theaters. There's a modest sense of optimism that even if a macro slowdown transpires, consumers may keep treating themselves to small and local excursions, and movies can be a reliable low-budget get-out-of-the-house entertainment option.

Larger fun and leisure expenses, such as sporting events and concerts, or protracted discretionary categories, like overseas vacations, could be the first to get slashed from household budgets.

This Year Tracks 2024’s YTD Ticket Sales

Source: IMDbPro Box Office Mojo

To cap off 2024, Disney (DIS) and Marvel's (MRVL) "Deadpool & Wolverine," Pixar's "Inside Out 2," Disney Animation's "Moana 2" and Universal's (UVV) "Wicked" collectively delivered a box office boost. Full-year ticket sales were lower year-on-year, however, reaching just $8.74 billion domestically in 2024.2

Disney snatched the crowd for the most movie-goer revenue, claiming 25% of US ticket sales.3 According to CNBC, 2025’s box office will likely fall shy of the $10 billion mark and remain below the $11 billion pre-COVID benchmark. Internationally, though, it’s a different tale.4

The Big China Story

“Ne Zha 2” has been an absolute blockbuster. Investors with their heads in the trading sand following the market’s day-to-day might not know about this one. Released on Valentine’s Day in the US, it has grossed a paltry $20.9 million. Totals from Europe, Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) and Asia Pacific (APAC) are unimpressive, too.5 But in China, it’s a gaudy gross at $1.86 billion, making global sales likely to top $2 billion before long.6

And that’s an important point - the movie industry isn’t centered within North American borders any longer. Indeed, US exceptionalism (in this instance) is a relic of the past. Moreover, the at-home entertainment dimension is a force to be reckoned with. Major players like Disney, Universal, and Paramount (PARA) continue to battle convenient streaming options dominated by Netflix (NFLX) and Amazon (AMZN).

Netflix Takes a Backseat

Our data show that Netflix may be chilling on its movie production pace. The $393 billion market cap Communication Services sector company stole the show in 2024. Over the next four months or so - through the July 4 holiday and much of the summer - it's slated to release just eight major motion pictures.

On the other hand, Lionsgate Entertainment (LGF.A, LGF.B) is expected to produce 11 big movies through early August. Comcast (CMCSA), Warner Bros, Disney, and Sony Group (SONY) are also prolific over the rest of the second quarter through early Q3.

Upcoming Movie Release Count

Source: Wall Street Horizon

“A Minecraft Movie” tops the 2025 box office, but there have been other success stories. Disney’s “Captain America: Brave New World” will almost certainly eclipse the $200 million mark Easter weekend, while “Mufasa: The Lion King” has notched $126 million in sales this year after tallying just under $100 million last December.7

New Releases

Entertainment analysts now look forward to fresh titles. “Captain America: Brave New World” debuted digitally earlier this week, along with Amazon’s “A Working Man” and Universal’s “A Woman in the Yard.” Netflix is slated to release iHostage on Good Friday; Warner Bros is banking on “Sinners” to score big numbers - it hits the silver screen and IMAX theaters on the 18th.

Twists and Turns to Pique All Interests

“Thunderbolts,” “The Accountant 2,” and “Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith” are some upcoming blockbusters that will hopefully wow audiences. Looking for a date-night flick? There are high hopes for “Pride & Prejudice,” a 20th-anniversary re-release, which comes out on April 20. Adam Sandler fans, your time will come; ”Happy Gilmore 2” hits the Netflix library on July 25.

Before that, TC fans will look forward to “Mission: Impossible: The Final Reckoning” on May 23. Meanwhile, the major Independence Day blockbuster may be “Jurassic World Rebirth.” Sticking with this '90s jaunt, “I Know What You Did Last Summer” aims to hook in horror-film aficionados beginning on July 18.

The Bottom Line

So, there’s really something for everyone in the months ahead. Theater traffic caught a boost recently after a sluggish start to 2025, and the international plot is even more compelling. This holiday weekend may kick off a busy spring and summer box office season. Consumer spending trends, just like some big forthcoming releases, will be watched.

1 Domestic Yearly Box Office, Box Office Mojo, April 15, 2025, Domestic Yearly Box Office
2 Domestic Yearly Box Office, Box Office Mojo, April 15, 2025, Domestic Yearly Box Office
3 Disney wins the 2024 box office as year-end receipts offer a welcome boost, CNBC, Sara Whitten, January 15, 2025, Disney wins the 2024 box office as year-end receipts offer a welcome boost
4 Wall Street is watching for a $10 billion domestic box office in 2026. Disney could get it there, CNBC, Sarah Whitten, February 2, 2025, https://www.cnbc.com
5 Ne Zha 2, Box Office Mojo, April 15, 2025, Ne Zha 2
6 Demon-child movie wows China – and smashes global box office records, The Guardian, Amy Hawkins, March 16, 2025, Demon-child movie wows China - and smashes global box office records
7 Domestic Yearly Box Office, Box Office Mojo, April 15, 2025, Domestic Yearly Box Office

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla: Sell Now Or Regret Later
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) is in a crisis mode as its sales are falling, the brand image is shattered, and the future looks bleak as a result of the ongoing trade war. The company’s shares are already down over 20% since we published our latest article on the company nearly two months ago, and we believe that the worst is not over yet and there’s still room to fall. We also don’t expect Tesla to report decent Q1 results on April 22, as its quarterly deliveries already took a major hit in recent months, while the increase in macroeconomic risks will likely limit its upside in the following months.

The Damage Is Done

The Q1 delivery numbers that were released earlier this month showed that Tesla is in a crisis mode. During the first quarter of the year, the company delivered only 336,681 vehicles, down from the estimates of 377,000 vehicles, which is one of the worst quarterly delivery numbers in recent years. What’s worse for Tesla is that its deliveries have fallen at a time when the EV market is booming, and its legacy peers are reporting a rise in EV sales in Q1. As a result of this, we don’t expect Tesla to release decent earnings results next week when plans to reveal its Q1 report.

While in part the decline could be attributed to the changeover of Model Y lines across some of its factories, it would be foolish to believe that Elon Musk’s involvement in politics had nothing to do with it. In recent months, there have been multiple protests against Elon Musk, which have negatively affected Tesla’s sales. In addition to that, J.P. Morgan stated that the damage to Tesla’s brand has been unprecedented and lowered the company’s price target to only $120 per share.

Although there’s a possibility that Elon Musk will step down from its role in the government, we believe that the damage to Tesla’s brand has been made, and it will be hard for the company to overcome the challenges that it faces. At the same time, the escalation of the global trade war will likely limit Tesla’s upside even more.

As of now, a significant portion of Chinese imports into the United States are tariffed at 145%, while the rest of imports are tariffed at up to 245%. At the same time, American imports into China are now tariffed at 125%. Such a sudden increase in trade barriers between the biggest economies in the world will more than likely undermine the growth of the global economy and will also affect Tesla’s sales and performance in the upcoming months. After all, the company’s production plans for Cybercab and Semi are already under a threat of disruption due to the rising cost of components.

In China, the future for Tesla doesn’t look bright as well. Before the escalation of the trade war by the Trump administration earlier this month, Tesla’s sales were already declining in China due to the rising competition and the ongoing price war there. Now the situation is likely to deteriorate further as Tesla stopped taking orders in China for its flagship models due to the escalation of the trade war.

In Europe, things are not going better. Tesla’s sales in major European markets have also mostly been declining largely because of Elon Musk’s involvement in politics not just in the United States but in some countries of the European Union as well. The company is now also required to pay a 7.8% tariff on its Chinese-made cars that are exported to the European Union, which will likely further undermine Tesla’s ability to perform well on the continent in the foreseeable future.

Largely because of all of those developments, Tesla has received numerous downward revisions in recent months. We believe that those revisions are justified and think that the upcoming earnings report for Q1 is going to disappoint investors considering the latest weak delivery numbers.

The Path To Recovery Is Still There

While Sino-American relations are unlikely to improve anytime soon, the potential pause of separate auto tariffs could be a good thing for Tesla. The company still sources a decent percentage of automotive parts from Mexico and Canada, while Elon Musk himself admitted that the tariffs will still have a significant impact on Tesla. That is why the potential pause could help the company avoid an outright disruption of its North American supply chain and somehow find a way to improve its sales back home.

Tesla’s bet on autonomy could also yield great results over time and help the company offset macro-related risks. In June, the company expects to release the unsupervised FSD offering in Austin, which should move Tesla closer to achieving its robotaxi goals over time. In addition to that, if the company manages to overcome the tariff-related challenges and reaches its goal of ramping up the production of autonomous Cybercabs next year, it will be able to improve its delivery numbers in the future.

Considering this, Tesla’s upcoming releases of autonomous products and features could nevertheless help the company mitigate some of the macro risks that it currently faces.

The Real Value of Tesla

The fall of deliveries along with the trade war made us change some of the assumptions in the valuation model ahead of the earnings release. The previous model showed that Tesla’s intrinsic value is $81.56 per share. Following the latest developments, we believe that the real value of Tesla is even lower.

The change of some of the assumptions is happening at a time when Tesla is trading at $254.11 per share, the forecast period still covers the next five years, the perpetual growth rate remains at 3%, and the cash and total debt numbers have been taken from the latest earnings report.

Tesla’s discount rate in our valuation model stands at 10.46%. We figured it out by calculating the cost of debt primarily by using the TTM numbers, and the cost of equity where the risk-free rate is 4.40%, the beta stands at 1.84, and the market return rate is 7.77%.

In the forecast table below, one of the biggest changes happened with the sales assumptions, which were decreased by 5% in comparison to the last model in all fiscal years. This is because the fall in deliveries shows that the sales momentum is waning, while the trade war will likely have a negative long-term effect on Tesla’s performance. For the current fiscal year, we expect only a 10% Y/Y growth rate in sales, which is similar to the latest expectations.

The EBIT margin is unchanged for now. This is because there’s a possibility that the upside from autonomy could offset the tariff-related downside in the upcoming months and years. At the same time, we wouldn’t rule out a major downward revision of margins after the Q1 results are released if the management decreases its own outlook, as it would likely signal that the impact from the trade war is going to be more meaningful than expected.

The bottom part of the forecast table mostly remained unchanged from the previous model.

This new model shows that Tesla’s intrinsic value is only $67.12 per share, below our previous calculations, and below the current price by around 73%. We wouldn’t consider it to be an unrealistic estimate of Tesla’s real value since under our updated assumptions the company is expected to make between $4 billion and $5 billion in FCF in 2025. This is already an increase from $3.6 billion in FCF last year. Also, Tesla currently trades at around 100 times its forward earnings while the sector’s median is around 13x, which shows that the company indeed is greatly overvalued and its stock price is significantly detached from reality.

Final Thoughts

Tesla faces so many challenges at once that we don’t see a path to a major recovery of its stock anytime soon. Because of the increased macroeconomic risks, its upside is now even more questionable, while the downside remains significant. That is why we don’t expect much from the upcoming earnings report that’s scheduled to be released on April 22 and continue to rate Tesla as a Sell.
